coredata.dc:title: Surface-charge-switch triggered self assembly of vancomycin modified carbon nanodots for enhanced photothermal eradication of vancomycin-resistant Enterococci biofilms
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Fang
Li, Qixian
Zhu, Jingru
Liu, Xinyue
Ding, Juan
Sun, Jie
Liu, Yang
Jiang, Tingting
coredata.dc:description: A new type of vancomycin (Van)-modified carbon nanodots (CNDs@Van) with pH-responsive surface charge switchable activity was successfully developed by covalently cross-linking Van on the surface of carbon nanodots (CNDs). Polymeric Van was formed on the surface of CNDs by covalent modification, which enhanced the targeted binding of CNDs@Van to vancomycin-resistant enterococci (VRE) biofilms and effectively reduced the carboxyl groups on the surface of CNDs to achieve pH-responsive surface charge switching. Most importantly, CNDs@Van was free at pH 7.4, but assembled at pH 5.5 owing to surface charge switching from negative to zero, resulting in remarkably enhanced near-infrared (NIR) absorption and photothermal properties. CNDs@Van exhibited good biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and weak hemolytic effects under physiological conditions (pH 7.4). Regarding targeted binding to VRE bacteria, CNDs@Van self-assembled in a weakly acidic environment (pH 5.5) generated by VRE biofilms, giving enhanced photokilling effects in in vitro and in vivo assays. Therefore, potentially, CNDs@Van can be used as a novel antimicrobial agent against VRE bacterial infections and their biofilms.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Vancomycin
Surface-charge-switch
Vancomycin-resistant Enterococci biofilms
Photothermal therapy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro photothermal eradication of VRE biofilms
In vivo photothermal eradication of VRE biofilms
In vitro photothermal eradication of VRE biofilms
In vivo photothermal eradication of VRE biofilms

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We appreciate the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province , China ( ZR2020MC079 , ZR2014BP004 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21505066 ), a project of Shandong Province Higher Educational Science and Technology Program , China ( J18KA265 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of vancomycin (Van) modified carbon nanodots (CNDs@Van). (a) Synthesis of CNDs@Van. (b) pH-responsive assembly of CNDs@Van in a weakly acid environment (pH 5.5) or VRE biofilm (c) in vivo photokilling effect of CNDs@Van against VRE biofilm infections.

The pH sensitivity of carbon nanodots (CNDs) and four types of CNDs@Van (0.5 mg/mL). (a): Photograph of the samples after centrifugation. After centrifugation, the CNDs remain free and present a yellow solution, which can mask the red color of the methyl red indicator in pH 4.0–5.5. The pH values were determined using the acid-base indicator methyl red. (b): Plot of absorbance at 808 nm versus pH values.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of CNDs ((a) and (b)) and CNDs@Van ((c) and (d)) at pH 7.4 and pH 5.5. It is noteworthy that the folded background in the TEM photographs was caused by salt molecules in the pH buffer rather than the amorphous carbon structure. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis of CNDs and CNDs@Van (e). Zeta-potential of CNDs and CNDs@Van (f). The CNDs@Van used here are CNDs@Van (100).

Photothermal performance of CNDs@Van. (a): Thermographic images of CNDs@Van (0.5 mg/mL) solution at varying irradiation times (808 nm, 2.0 W/cm2). (b): Temperature changes of CNDs@Van (0.5 mg/mL, pH 5.5) under repeated on/off NIR irradiation (808 nm, 2.0 W/cm2). (c): Temperature variation of CNDs@Van (0.5 mg/mL, pH 5.5) at different laser powers. (d): Calculated photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of CNDs@Van at pH 5.5. Red dots: temperature change curve of CNDs@Van (0.5 mg/mL, pH 5.5) at one on/off NIR irradiation. Blue dots and black lines: we plot the negative natural logarithm of the driving temperature (-Ln (θ)) and the cooling time to calculate the time constants (τs) and η.

(a)–(f): Live/dead bacterial fluorescent staining of vancomycin-resistant enterococci (VRE) biofilm after the treatment of CNDs or CNDs@Van, where (a)–(e) are fields of view for SYTO9 and (f) is a field of view for PI. (g)–(I): 3D plot of fluorescence intensity versus position. The green and red images show alive and dead bacterial cells, respectively. The final concentration of CNDs and CNDs@Van was 0.5 mg/mL, and NIR irradiation was performed by an 808 nm laser at 2.0 W/cm2 for 10 min.

Flow cytometry analysis of VRE biofilm treated with PBS, CNDs, or CNDs@Van, respectively. The final concentration of CNDs and CNDs@Van was 0.5 mg/mL. NIR irradiation was performed by an 808 nm laser at 2.0 W/cm2 for 10 min.

(a): Photographs of bacterial culture plates after treatment with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), CNDs, or CNDs@Van, respectively. (b): Bacterial survival of VRE biofilms after treatment with different concentrations of CNDs@Van NIR irradiation. (c)–(d): Bacterial viability (c) and growth curves (d) of VRE biofilm treated with various treatments including PBS in the dark, PBS under NIR irradiation, CNDs in the dark, CNDs under NIR irradiation, CNDs@Van in the dark and CNDs@Van under NIR irradiation. The final concentration of CNDs and CNDs@Van was 0.5 mg/mL, and near-infrared (NIR) irradiation was performed by an 808 nm laser at 2.0 W/cm2 for 10 min **p < 0.01.

In vivo photothermal treatment of CNDs@Van. (a) Photothermal imaging of mice treated with PBS or CNDs@Van after different NIR irradiation times. (b) Photographs, schematic diagrams, and H&E staining of infected tissues with different treatments are shown from the figure’s left to right. The final concentration of CNDs and CNDs@Van was 0.5 mg/mL, and NIR irradiation was performed by an 808 nm laser at 2.0 W/cm2 for 10 min.

head.ce:title: Surface-charge-switch triggered self assembly of vancomycin modified carbon nanodots for enhanced photothermal eradication of vancomycin-resistant Enterococci biofilms

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Ludong University, Yantai 264025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life Sciences, Ludong University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Sciences, Ludong University, Yantai 264025, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Fang Zhang and Qixian Li contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new type of vancomycin (Van)-modified carbon nanodots (CNDs@Van) with pH-responsive surface charge switchable activity was successfully developed by covalently cross-linking Van on the surface of carbon nanodots (CNDs). Polymeric Van was formed on the surface of CNDs by covalent modification, which enhanced the targeted binding of CNDs@Van to vancomycin-resistant enterococci (VRE) biofilms and effectively reduced the carboxyl groups on the surface of CNDs to achieve pH-responsive surface charge switching. Most importantly, CNDs@Van was free at pH 7.4, but assembled at pH 5.5 owing to surface charge switching from negative to zero, resulting in remarkably enhanced near-infrared (NIR) absorption and photothermal properties. CNDs@Van exhibited good biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and weak hemolytic effects under physiological conditions (pH 7.4). Regarding targeted binding to VRE bacteria, CNDs@Van self-assembled in a weakly acidic environment (pH 5.5) generated by VRE biofilms, giving enhanced photokilling effects in in vitro and in vivo assays. Therefore, potentially, CNDs@Van can be used as a novel antimicrobial agent against VRE bacterial infections and their biofilms.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Vancomycin (Van) is considered a last resort for the treatment of intractable multi-drug resistant infections induced by Gram-positive bacteria. However, vancomycin-resistant enterococci (VRE) has emerged and posed an increasingly serious threat to public health [1–5]. Van resistance was developed based on the structural transformation of peptidoglycan terminals from D-Ala to D-Lac, which dramatically decreased the binding affinity between Van and bacterial cell membranes [6]. In addition to the structural transformation, Van resistance is derived from biofilms, which are hybrids of bacterial cells packed in self-produced matrices of extracellular polymeric substances (EPS). Owing to the encapsulation of EPS, oxygen cannot penetrate biofilms, and bacteria are forced to undergo anaerobic glycolysis to form a weakly acidic environment, limiting antibiotic utilization in the biofilms and promoting bacterial drug resistance [7–11]. Therefore, it is essential to develop new strategies for eradicating VRE bacteria and their mature biofilms.

Photothermal therapy (PTT), as a bactericidal modality, can efficiently convert light energy into local heat to rupture cell membranes and denature intracellular proteins, resulting in the death of drug-resistant bacteria [12–17]. PTT has attracted attention due to its broad-spectrum bactericidal activity, short treatment time (only a few minutes), non-releasing properties, and noninvasive treatment [18,19]. More importantly, PTT is effective in preventing the development of drug resistance because the protein denaturation produced by PTT completely prevents the structural transformation or genetic mutation that occurs in drug-resistant bacteria [20]. Consequently, nanocomposites have been developed for antimicrobial PTT. However, serious damage to the healthy tissues caused by non-localized heat remains a significant problem for the PTT of biofilms. To solve this problem, a variety of surface-adaptive nanomaterials have been developed for enhancing the targeting and permeability of bacterial biofilms through specific responses to low-pH environments [11,19,21–25]. For example, Ji et al. reported surface-adaptive mixed charged zwitterionic gold nanoparticles that can specifically aggregate in the acidic environment of Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) biofilms to produce a significantly enhanced photokilling effect [11]. Luo et al. also reported that zwitterion-modified gold nanorods with pendant carboxyl betaine groups are positively charged on the surface in an acidic environment, resulting in an enhanced binding affinity and photokilling effect toward MRSA and Escherichia coli (E. coli) biofilms [19]. In addition, Xiao et al. constructed ciprofloxacin-conjugated gold nanorods with pH-induced surface charge transformable activity, where lower pH and overexpression of lipase accelerated the release of ciprofloxacin in the MRSA biofilms, and the surface charge of the gold nanorods shifted from negative to positive, producing enhanced photothermal effects [22].

As a fascinating class of nanocarbon structures, carbon nanodots (CNDs) are discrete quasi-spherical nanoparticles smaller than 10 nm in size. They are photo-bleaching resistant, size-dependent luminescence, easy to produce and bio-conjugated, and have cheap and abundant raw materials. In the past few years, many significant advances have taken place in the field of CNDs applications [26,27]. Therefore, CNDs have been widely applied in biological imaging and detection owing to their good solubility, low cytotoxicity, and excellent biocompatibility [21,28–34]. More importantly, numerous studies have shown that the self-assembly of CNDs can be used to obtain more attractive photothermal properties [35–39]. For example, CNDs can be assembled by hydrogen bonding to enhance near-infrared (NIR) absorption and improve photothermal conversion efficiency at 732 nm (η = 50 %) [38]. Assembly of multifunctional CNDs was also designed with Fe3+-driven Fenton reaction, exhibiting excellent photothermal properties [36]. Moreover, Xu et al. also reported a kind of smart CNDs based on electrostatic attraction generated by surface protonation, which has a low pH-driven self-assembly feature [37]. At pH 4.7, smart CNDs demonstrate enhanced NIR absorption at 650 nm and photothermal conversion efficiency (η = 42.13 %) [37]. The surface charge can be observed as an important factor for keeping the CND free, and its changes can promote the self-assembly of the CNDs and produce enhanced photothermal effects.

Herein, we designed a new type of vancomycin (Van)-modified carbon nanodots (CNDs@Van) with surface charge-switchable properties ( Fig. 1). According to the multivalent theory, the polymeric Van produces higher binding affinity for VRE biofilms by specifically binding to D-Ala-D-Lac at the peptidoglycan terminus of the VRE bacterial cell wall [40–42]. Here, Van was modified on the surface of CNDs to form a polymer pattern that improves its specific binding capacity to VRE biofilms [40,41]. In addition, Van's amino group is covalently crosslinked with the carboxyl group on the surface of CNDs, which can effectively reduce the negative charge on the surface of CNDs. The as-prepared CNDs@Van is negatively charged in a neutral pH solution and has good dispersibility. However, in the presence of the VRE biofilms, CNDs@Van with a small particle size could effectively penetrate the biofilms and bind specifically to them. Owing to the low-pH environment of the biofilms, the negative charge on the surface of CNDs@Van can change from the original COO- to COOH, resulting in zero charge on the surface of CNDs@Van. Owing to the disappearance of the negative charge on the surface, CNDs@Van can assemble in situ to form aggregated particles and exhibit significantly enhanced photokilling effects on the VRE biofilms.

The CNDs were synthesized based on the pyrolysis method reported by Ortega-Munoz et al. [43]. The anhydrous citric acid (10 mg) was placed in a round-bottom flask and heated in an oil bath at 180 °C for 3 h. The resulting black solid was dissolved in distilled water for dialysis (3500 D cutoff, 24 h) and lyophilized.

CNDs (20 mg) and sulfoxide chloride (SOCl2 4 mL) were heated and refluxed at 80 °C for 48 h. The unreacted SOCl2 was removed by rotary evaporation and residues were dried at 60 °C for 2 h. Since the number of carboxyl groups on the surface of CNDs is more than 15 times that of amino groups in Van, four CNDs@Van with different Van contents were synthesized to investigate the effect of Van modification on the aggregates of CNDs, where the content of CNDs was 20 mg and those of Van were 25, 50, 75, and 100 mg, respectively. Therefore, acyl chloride-activated CNDs (20 mg) were mixed with Van (25, 50, 75, and 100 mg) and triethylamine (TEA, 7, 14, 21, and 28 µL) in anhydrous N'N-dimethyl amide (DMF, 3 mL). The reaction mixture was vigorously stirred at room temperature for 12 h and then ether was added to precipitate the product. After washing with ether and ethanol three times, the products were dried in vacuum to obtain CNDs@Van (20), CNDs@Van (50), CNDs@Van (75), and CNDs@Van (100). The number in parentheses is the mass of Van added during CNDs@Van synthesis in mg.

Aqueous solutions of CNDs and CNDs@Van were added dropwise into a molybdenum-coated copper grid (200 mesh) and dried in air. The samples were imaged at 100 kV using a transmission electron microscope (TEM; Talos F200x, FEI, Holland). The CNDs and CNDs@Van were also studied using a Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Magna550, Nicolet, USA). Energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) was performed using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, SU8010, Hitachi, Japan) under vacuum at an accelerating voltage of 15 kV. The diameters and zeta potentials of CNDs@Van were determined in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, 10 mM, pH 5.5, or pH 7.4) using dynamic light scattering (DLS, Malvern ZetaSizer ZS2000, UK). Aqueous solutions of CNDs and CNDs@Van (2.0 mg/mL) were placed in a quartz cell with a 5 mm path to record the ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) absorption spectra. A fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse, Agilent, Japan) was used to record the fluorescence spectra of the CNDs and CNDs@Van (0.5 mg/mL).

Various samples (H2O, CNDs, CNDs@Van(20), CNDs@Van(50), CNDs@Van(75), and CNDs@Van(100)) were added to different PBS buffers at pH values from 4.0 to 6.5, which were determined using the acid-base indicator methyl red. The final concentrations of the CNDs, CNDs@Van, and methyl red were 0.5 mg/mL, 0.5 mg/mL, and 10.0 µg/mL, respectively. After centrifugation (12,000 rpm, 10 min), photographs of the centrifuge tubes were obtained to evaluate the assembly performance of CNDs@Van.

The assemblies of various samples were also quantitatively examined by changes in absorbance at 808 nm. A spectrometer (TU-1810PC, Pujinjie, China) was used to collect UV-Vis spectra of various CNDs@Van samples in different PBS buffers.

The photothermal performance of CNDs@Van in different PBS buffers (pH 5.5 or pH 7.4) was examined under NIR irradiation. An aqueous solution of CNDs@Van (0.5 mg/mL) was placed in a glass vial (1.5 mL) and irradiated with an 808 nm NIR laser (2.0 W/cm2, 10 min). Thermocouple microprobes (UT325, UNI-T, China) were used to record temperature changes. Infrared thermal images were collected using a thermal imager (Compact pro, Seek, USA).

Calculation of the photothermal conversion efficiency. According to the previous work [44], the photothermal conversion efficiency (η) was calculated via the following equation: η = (hS(Tmax-Tsurr)-Qdis)/I(1–10-A808), in which h is the heat transfer coefficient, S is the surface area of the cell, Tmax is the maximum steady-state temperature, Tsurr is the ambient temperature of the surrounding, Qdis represents the heat dissipated from light absorption, I is the laser power, and A808 is the absorbance of CNDs@Van (pH 5.5) at 808 nm. Then, we calculated y according to the below equation: θ = (T-Tsurr)/(Tmas-Tsurr),where θ is the driving force temperature of the CNDs@Van at pH 5.5, (Tmax-Tsurr) represents the temperature change at the maximum steady-state temperature, and (T-Tsurr) represents the temperature increase compared to the surrounding. Another twp equations were also used, τs = -t/lnθ, where τs represents the time constant. hS = msCs/τs, where ms and Cs represent the mass and heat capacity of pure water, respectively. According to the equations above mentioned, the time constant for heat transfer from the CNDs@Van at pH 5.5 was determined to be τs = 591.2 s by applying the linear time data from the cooling period vs negative natural logarithm of driving force temperature. And the photothermal conversion efficiency was calculated to be 33.32 %.

Vancomycin-resistant enterococci (VRE, ATCC51299) were cultured in a liquid tryptic soy broth (TSB) medium at 37 °C. After overnight culturing, the VRE bacteria cells were washed three times with PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4) and the concentration of the bacterial solution was adjusted to a concentration of 1 × 108 colony forming unit (CFU)/mL. The VRE suspension (200 µL, 1 × 108 CFU/mL) was incubated into a 96-well plate and cultured at 37 °C for 48 h. TSB media were refreshed every 24 h. The matured biofilms were gently wash washed three times with PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 5.5) for subsequent assays.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro photothermal eradication of VRE biofilms
ce:section.ce:para: We performed crystal violet (CV) staining assays of VRE biofilms to determine the biofilm eradication activity of CNDs and CNDs@Van [45]. VRE biofilms were washed three times with PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 5.5), and then 150 µL of PBS buffer and 50 µL of CNDs or CNDs@Van solution were added. The final concentration of both CNDs and CNDs@Van was 0.5 mg/mL. After incubation at 37 °C for 24 h, NIR or dark treatment was conducted for photothermal eradication of VRE biofilms. After the removal of non-adherent bacterial cells and CNDs or CNDs@Van using PBS buffer, CV staining was used to evaluate the biomass of the VRE biofilms. First, 200 µL of methanol was added for 15 min to fix the biofilms. After the removal of formaldehyde, 100 µL CV solution (0.1 %) was added to stain biofilms for 5 min. After washing with PBS buffer, 200 uL of acetic acid solution (33.3 %) was incubated with the biofilm for 30 min. Finally, the biomass of the VRE biofilm was quantified by measuring the absorbance at 570 nm using a microplate reader (Multiskan FC, Thermo Scientific, USA). All samples were performed in triplicate for independent experiments.

To evaluate the in vitro photokilling effect of CNDs@Van, live/dead bacterial fluorescent staining was performed according to a previous study by Wu et al. [45]. After washing with 10% HCl and 10 % NaOH, glass slides (1.5 cm × 1.5 cm) were placed on a 6-well plate. VRE suspension (2 mL, 1 × 108 CFU/mL) was incubated in a 6-well plate and cultured at 37 °C for 48 h. TSB media were refreshed every 24 h. After washing with PBS buffer, VRE biofilms treated with different samples (PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 5.5), CNDs (0.5 mg/mL), or CNDs@Van (0.5 mg/mL)) were incubated in the dark or were irradiated with a NIR laser (808 nm, 2.0 W/cm2) for 10 min. Next, VRE biofilms were washed with 0.85% NaCl solution and stained with a mixture solution of SYTO-9 (2.5 μM) and propidium iodide (PI, 15 μM) in the dark for 15 min. After washing with 0.85 % NaCl, the biofilms with various treatments were imaged using a fluorescent microscope (Axioskop2, ZEISS, German). Flow cytometry was also used to analyze the bacterial viability of the VRE biofilms after the photokilling of CNDs@Van. After various treatments, the VRE biofilms were washed with 0.85 % NaCl and stained with the mixture solutions of SYTO-9 and PI. After incubation in the dark for 15 min, a flow cytometer (FACSymphony, BD, Biosciences, USA) was used to examine the stained bacteria (1 × 108 CFU/mL). The flow cytometer used a 488 nm laser with 590 nm and 610 nm filters to collect green and red emissions.

Next, standard plate counting assays and growth curve plotting were achieved for the quantitative analysis of the in vitro photokilling effect of CNDs@Van. After treatment with PBS buffer, CNDs (0.5 mg/mL) or CNDs@Van (0.5 mg/mL), VRE biofilms were incubated in the dark or were irradiated under a NIR laser (808 nm, 0.8 W/cm2, 10 min). After washing with PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4), the VRE biofilms were pipetted and vigorously vortexed to disperse them. The VRE bacterial solution was then diluted (102-fold) in PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4) and the samples (50 µL) were separately spread on solid TSB agar plates. After incubation at 37 °C for 16 h, we photographed and counted the colony-forming units (CFUs) formed on the plates. In addition, after various treatments, 50 µL of diluted VRE bacterial solution was added to TSB liquid medium (30 mL, pH 7.4) and incubated at 37 °C with 200 rpm shaking. The OD600 values of the bacterial solutions were recorded every 1 h and the bacterial growth curves were plotted.

We conducted a 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assays to determine the cytotoxicity and phototoxicity of CNDs and CNDs@Van [40]. 5 × 104 3T3 cells were cultured overnight in 96-well plates in 5 % CO2 at 37 °C. Cells were then incubated with CNDs or CNDs@Van (0–0.5 mg/mL) in the dark for 30 min. Cells were then treated with or without 808 nm irradiation for 10 min. The power of irradiation was 0.8 W/cm2. Next, cells were incubated with MTT solution for 4 h, and Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added to dissolve the formazan crystals from the alive cells. Cell viability was analyzed by measuring the absorbance at 490 nm, and in vitro hemolysis of CNDs@Van was evaluated using the murine red blood cells (RBCs) according to a previously reported method [19,46]. After washing with PBS buffer three times, the RBCs solution (900 µL) was incubated with 100 µL aqueous solution of distilled water (H2O), PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4), CNDs or CNDs@Van at 37 °C for 1 h. After centrifugation (1000 rpm, 10 min), the supernatant was diluted five times with PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4) to measure the absorbance at 492 nm. The hemolysis was calculated based on the following formula: hemolysis percentage (%) = (OD sample − OD negative) ÷ (OD positive − OD negative) × 100 % [19,46,47]. PBS buffer and H2O served as negative and positive samples, respectively.

In vivo biosafety of CNDs@Van was also evaluated using haematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining assays. Female BALB/C mice weighing 16–22 g were anesthetised with isoflurane to depilate their hind limbs. Then, 100 µL of an aqueous solution of 0.5 mg/mL CNDs@Van was subcutaneously injected into the hind limbs of mice. PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 5.5) was used as a control group. The injection sites were incubated in the dark or were irradiated (808 nm, 0.8 W/cm2, 10 min). After three days of treatments, five major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) were histologically analyzed using H&E staining assays.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo photothermal eradication of VRE biofilms
ce:section.ce:para: The Ethics Committee of Ludong University approved all animal experiments. Female BALB/C mice weighing 16–22 g were anaesthetized using isoflurane to depilate their hind limbs. VRE biofilms were washed and diluted with PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 5.5) to obtain a VRE bacterial solution at 1 × 108 CFU/mL. After subcutaneous injection of VRE bacteria (2 × 106 CFU), PBS buffer or CNDs@Van with a final concentration of 0.5 mg/mL were injected in situ at the infected site and incubated in the dark or irradiated with 808 nm laser of 0.8 W/cm2 for 10 min. Infrared thermal images of the mice were collected using a thermal imager (Compact pro, Seek, USA). The infection regression and body weight changes of the mice were also photographed at 24-h intervals to assess the antimicrobial activity and biosafety of CNDs@Van. After three days of treatment, the infected mice were sacrificed. Infected sites were cut down and homogenized in PBS buffer containing 1 % Triton X-100. After dilution, the solution was plated onto TSB agar plates. The number of viable bacteria in the infected tissue was assessed using a plate counting approach. After three days of treatment, the infected sites were removed for H&E staining and histological analysis.

Extensive research has shown CNDs fabrication using a variety of strategies and carbon sources [48]. Citric acid is often used for the bottom-up fabrication of CNDs via one-step carbonization owing to its advantages of being small, green, low-cost, and efficient [43]. In this work, we synthesised anhydride-coated CNDs by anhydrous pyrolysis of pure citric acid and used sulfoxide chloride to activate the carboxyl groups for further functionalization and decoration. The amino groups of the Van molecules can be covalently attached to the active acid chloride groups of the CNDs to form CNDs@Van containing polymeric Van. As shown in Fig. S1, the as-prepared CNDs@Van had a specific absorption peak at 280 nm, which is a typical absorption of Van molecules (Fig. S1(a)). The size distribution of the as-prepared CNDs@Van was 3–5 nm (Fig. S1(b)). From the EDS spectra, CNDs contained two major elements as follows: carbon and oxygen. In addition, a third major element, nitrogen, was present in CNDs@Van, which was derived from Van molecules containing amino groups (Fig. S1(c)). In addition, the as-prepared CNDs@Van showed FTIR absorption peaks at 900–1200 cm-1 and 1480 cm-1, which coincided with the vibrational peaks of the C-N bond of Van and the CO bond of CNDs, respectively (Fig. S1(d)). These results indicate that Van was successfully covalently modified at the surface of the CNDs using the chlorination reaction to form CNDs@Van, which provides a new idea for other modifications of CNDs.

In addition, we investigated the binding behavior of CNDs@Van to VRE bacteria using TEM images. Unlike CNDs, most CNDs@Van were present on the surface of the VRE bacteria rather than being dispersed around the bacterial cells, demonstrating the significant binding properties of CNDs@Van to VRE bacteria (Fig. S2). This could be related to the polymeric Van modified on the surface of CNDs, which has a significantly enhanced affinity for the VRE bacterial cell wall according to multivalent theory.

To investigate the effect of Van modification on the self-assembly of CNDs, we synthesized four types of CNDs@Van with Van dosages of 25, 50, 75, and 100 mg. These four types of CNDs@Van were named CNDs@Van(25), CNDs@Van(50), CNDs@Van(75), and CNDs@Van(100). After purification, the four types of CNDs@Van were tested for pH sensitivity. Methyl red was used as an indicator for weakly acidic environments (pH 4.0–6.5) and centrifugation was performed to assess the aggregates of CNDs@Van in different pH environments. As shown in Fig. 2(a), when the pH value was increased from 4.0 to 6.5, no significant assembly was observed in the CNDs group. Moreover, the yellow color of free CNDs masked the red color of the methyl red at pH 4.0–5.5. However, the four types of CNDs@Van exhibited significant self-assembly, which was enhanced by decreasing the pH. Moreover, CNDs@Van (100) showed more precipitations at pH 5.5 compared to the other three types of CNDs@Van, indicating its effective assembly in the weakly acidic environment of biofilms. UV-Vis spectroscopy was used to quantify the aggregates of CNDs@Van in different pH environments. Fig. 2(b) shows the variation curves of the absorbance values of CNDs@Van at 808 nm in different pH environments. Compared with CNDs, the absorbance values of the four types of CNDs@Van at 808 nm increased with decreasing of pH values. Moreover, the absorbance value of CNDs@Van (100) at pH 5.5 was noticeably higher than that of the other three types of CNDs@Van, indicating its stronger response to the low pH environment of VRE biofilms. Therefore, all CNDs@Van involved in subsequent studies are referred to as CNDs@Van(100). Next, we compared the UV-Vis spectra of CNDs and CNDs@Van between pH 5.5 and 7.4. As shown in Fig. S3, the absorption of CNDs in the NIR region did not vary with pH. However, the NIR absorption of CNDs@Van was sharply enhanced when the pH was decreased to 5.5, which is consistent with the data in Fig. 2.

To explain the changes in UV-Vis spectra of CNDs@Van in different pH environments, assays of TEM, DLS, and zeta-potential were performed, which also clarified the changes in particle size and surface potential of CNDs@Van in different pH environments. As shown in Fig. 3(a) and (c), the size distribution of the as-prepared CNDs was 3–5 nm, which is similar to CNDs@Van. More importantly, the CNDs were free at both pH 5.5 and 7.4. CNDs@Van was free at pH 7.4, but significantly assembled at pH 5.5 (Fig. 3(a)–(d)), which is consistent with the DLS results (Fig. 3(e)). We speculated that the self-assembly of CNDs@Van was related to pH-induced changes in the surface charge of the nanoparticles. Therefore, we tested the zeta potentials of CNDs and CNDs@Van for different pH values. As depicted in Fig. 3(f), the zeta potential of CNDs at pH 7.4 (∼ − 20 mV) was much higher than that at pH 5.5 (∼ − 8 mV), indicating that a significant decrease in the negative charge on the surface of CNDs with decreasing pH values. Similar results were observed in the CNDs@Van group. It is noteworthy that CNDs@Van was negatively charged at pH 7.4 with a zeta-potential of approximately − 12 mV, while it was uncharged at pH 5.5 with a zeta-potential of 0 mV. Thus, CNDs@Van can be observed to have a negative surface charge at pH 7.4, generating a repulsive effect of negative charge to keep them free. However, when the pH dropped to 5.5, the net surface charge of CNDs@Van was almost zero and the disappearance of the charge repulsion allowed the nanoparticles to assemble. Interestingly, in contrast to the aggregates of CNDs@Van at pH 5.5, CNDs remained free in a weakly acidic environment, which can be attributed to more surface carboxyl groups of CNDs than CNDs@Van. When CNDs were in a weakly acidic environment, some of the carboxyl groups changed from COO- to COOH, but some of them still existed as COO-, making the net charge of the CNDs negative. The negative charge repulsion allowed the CNDs to be freely dispersed in aqueous solutions. However, for CNDs@Van, the covalent modification of Van significantly reduced the number of carboxyl groups on the surface of the CNDs. When CNDs@Van was in a weakly acidic environment, all the carboxyl groups on its surface changed from COO- to COOH, resulting in a net charge change from negative to zero. Thus, the Van molecules, modified on the surface of CNDs, enhanced their binding affinity to VRE biofilms through multivalent interactions and reduced the carboxyl groups on the surface of CNDs to promote zero net charges of CNDs@Van in environments below pH 5.5. It can be seen that affinity and pH targeting of VRE biofilms can be achieved by simple Van covalent modification. We believe that the simple strategy reported in this study can provide constructive suggestions for the development of other targeted nanocomplexes.

Since CNDs@Van undergoes surface charge loss in a low-pH environment, the disappearance of charge repulsion on the particle surface allows CNDs@Van to self-assemble. According to previous studies, the self-assembly of CNDs usually enhanced the photothermal properties [35–39]. Therefore, we investigated the photothermal performance of CNDs@Van in various pH environments. As shown in Fig. 4(a) and Fig. S4(a), the temperature of CNDs@Van solution at pH 5.5 increased obviously with an increase in the irradiation time and reached 58 °C after 5 min of irradiation, exceeding the pH 7.4 group by 16 °C. This result showed that CNDs@Van remained low photothermal performance in a neutral environment (pH 7.4), whereas it had a significantly enhanced photothermal effect in a weakly acidic environment (pH 5.5). Moreover, the solution temperature of CNDs@Van at pH 5.5 remarkably increased with the concentration of CNDs@Van and laser intensity (Figs. 4(c) and S2 (b)–(c)), exhibiting a photothermal conversion efficiency of 33.32 % (Fig. 4(d)). Notably, we did not observe substantial regression in the on/off temperature profile of CNDs@Van, demonstrating that the good photostability and thermal stability of CNDs@Van allowed for recovery and reuse in PTT applications (Fig. 4(b)). Therefore, based on these results, we can speculate that the as-prepared CNDs@Van can self-assemble in the weakly acidic environment (pH 5.5) provided by the VRE biofilms due to pH-responsive surface charge switching. It should be noted that CNDs@Van remain free in the neutral environment of healthy tissues to render lower photothermal features, but self-assemble in the VRE biofilms to yield enhanced photothermal properties. In other words, the enhanced photothermal performance of CNDs@Van is driven by the VRE biofilms, which can effectively reduce their non-targeted damage to healthy tissues.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro photothermal eradication of VRE biofilms
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm this hypothesis, we explored the photothermal performance and photokilling effect of CNDs@Van in VRE biofilms under in vitro conditions. Firstly, photothermal imaging was used to verify the photothermal performance of CNDs@Van in the VRE biofilms. We used a powerful laser intensity (2.0 W/cm2) in subsequent in vitro experiments to effectively demonstrate the in vitro photothermal performance of CNDs@Van in VRE biofilms. As shown in Fig. S5 (a) and (b), the VRE biofilm treated with CNDs@Van could reach 55 °C after NIR irradiation, which was higher than the PBS and CNDs groups. More importantly, the CV color intensity of the VRE biofilms was significantly reduced after NIR treatment of the CNDs@Van. However, no significant color intensity changes were observed in the CNDs-treated biofilms (Fig. S5(a)). The biomass statistics of the corresponding biofilms further confirmed that the NIR-treated CNDs@Van almost eliminated the bacterial cells from the VRE biofilms (Fig. S5(c)). These results indicate that CNDs@Van can effectively assemble in the weakly acidic environment provided by VRE biofilms and exhibit significantly enhanced photothermal eradication of VRE biofilms.

Subsequently, we performed live/dead bacterial fluorescent staining and flow cytometry assays to qualitatively assess bacterial viability. Bac-Light viability kits containing two types of fluorescent dyes were used for bacterial staining, where SYTO-9 and propidium iodide (PI) were used for live and dead bacteria, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5, obvious green fluorescence was observed in the PBS and CNDs groups, indicating no significant photokilling effect of CNDs on the VRE biofilms. However, the NIR group of CNDs@Van demonstrated noticeable red staining, distinguishing from the green staining of the dark group, indicating that CNDs@Van treated VRE biofilms were able to survive in the dark and were effectively killed upon NIR irradiation. Notably, flow cytometry assays showed similar fluorescence staining results ( Fig. 6). In the dark groups, approximately 5 % of the bacterial cells were stained red in the CNDs@Van group, which was slightly higher than in the PBS and CNDs groups (less than 2 %). The slight bactericidal effect of CNDs@Van in the dark may be related to the polymeric Van on the surface of CNDs, inhibiting bacterial growth by effectively binding to the cell wall of VRE bacteria. However, approximately 44 % of the bacteria were stained red after treatment with CNDs@Van and NIR irradiation, much higher than the data for the PBS and CNDs groups (less than 2 %). This result indicates that CNDs@Van has a good photokilling effect on VRE biofilms.

We then performed standard plate counting assays to quantify the photokilling effect of CNDs@Van on the VRE biofilms. As shown in Fig. 7(a) and (c), compared with three dark groups (PBS, CNDs, and CNDs@Van) and two NIR groups (PBS and CNDs), more than about 99 % of the bacteria were killed after CNDs@Van and NIR treatments. When the concentration of CNDs@Van reached 0.5 mg/mL, the bacterial survival rate was significantly reduced (Fig. 7(b)). We observed significant inhibition of VRE bacterial growth in the NIR group of CNDs@Van, but not in the other groups (Fig. 7(d)). Thus, it can be seen that CNDs@Van showed no apparent toxicity to VRE biofilms in the dark, but displayed a better photothermal eradication of biofilms than CNDs.

Before in vivo photothermal treatment, the biosafety and biocompatibility of CNDs@Van were assessed using in vitro MTT assays, hemolysis tests, and in vivo histological analysis. Notably, we used a low-intensity laser (0.8 W/cm2) for NIR irradiation in both in vitro MTT experiments and in vivo histological analysis to ensure cellular and skin tolerance to the NIR laser. As depicted in Fig. S6(a), in the dark groups, most cells survived after treatment with CNDs and CNDs@Van. Although cell viability decreased slightly after treatment with CNDs@Van and NIR, an effective lethal concentration (EC50) of 50 % was still not obtained at the highest concentration of 0.5 mg/mL. Fig. S6(b) shows the hemolysis of CNDs and CNDs@Van, in which no hemoglobin release was observed at concentrations up to 0.5 mg/mL. Thus, it can be seen that CNDs@Van have negligible cytotoxicity and phototoxicity, and its good biocompatibility can be used for in vivo photothermal therapy studies.

We analyzed the H&E staining results for five major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) to assess the in vivo biosafety of CNDs@Van. There were no significant inflammatory factors in the five major organs of the mice treated with CNDs@Van (Fig. S7). Similar results were obtained in the PBS group, suggesting that CNDs@Van did not pathologically harm the major organs of the mice. In addition, no weight loss was observed in the mice treated with CNDs@Van (Fig. S8(a)). These data then confirm the good biosafety of CNDs@Van for in vivo antimicrobial photothermal therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo photothermal eradication of VRE biofilms
ce:section.ce:para: Next, BALB/C mice were used to establish a subcutaneous infection model for in vivo photothermal treatment. We used a 0.8 W/cm2 laser for in vivo NIR treatment to ensure that the dermis was not damaged. As shown in Fig. 8(a), we collected thermographic images of VRE-infected mice. In the groups of NIR irradiation, the temperature of the CNDs@Van treated group (∼ 50 °C) was significantly higher than that of the PBS group. As shown in the left part of Fig. 8(b), we found significant abscesses in all three control groups (PBS + Dark, PBS+NIR, and CNDs@Van+Dark), and the abscesses persisted until the third day after treatment. However, after treatment with CNDs@Van and NIR irradiation, the infected abscess was significantly reduced until it was almost completely eradicated on the third day (Fig. S8(b)). In addition, H&E staining was also used to analyze the differences in inflammation at the infected site after different treatments. As shown in the right part of Fig. 8(b), although some inflammatory cells were present in the skin tissue after CNDs@Van and NIR irradiation, they were significantly fewer than those of the three controls. In addition, photokilling efficiency was quantified using the standard plate counting method. After treatment with CNDs@Van and NIR irradiation, the number of surviving bacteria was almost 102 times less than that in the three control groups (PBS + Dark, PBS+NIR, and CNDs@Van + Dark) (Fig. S8(c)). As can be seen, the outstanding in vitro photokilling effect can be translated into in vivo antimicrobial treatment, leading to the regression or even disappearance of infectious abscesses.

In summary, we prepared CNDs@Van with pH-responsive surface charge switchable activity by covalently cross-linking Van to the surface of CNDs, which can form polymeric derivatives to enhance the binding affinity of CNDs to VRE biofilms and also effectively reduce the carboxyl groups on the surface of CNDs to achieve pH-responsive surface charge switching. Owing to the negative surface charge, CNDs@Van remained free under physiological conditions (pH 7.4), resulting in good biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity and hemolytic effect, and low photothermal properties. However, when CNDs@Van specifically binds to the VRE biofilms, the weakly acidic environment (pH 5.5) provided by the biofilms switches the surface charge from negative to zero. The disappearance of charge repulsion drives the self-assembly of CNDs@Van in the VRE biofilms to produce significantly enhanced photothermal properties. Notably, the results of in vitro and in vivo assays verified that CNDs@Van can achieve satisfactory photothermal eradication of VRE biofilms and have good biosafety for normal cells and tissues. Thus, the CNDs@Van developed in this work have good biocompatibility and biosafety and have great potential for photothermal therapy of VRE bacteria and their biofilms.

Fang Zhang: Investigation, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Junyu Xia: Investigation, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Jingru Zhu: Formal analysis, Validation. Xinyue Liu: Formal analysis, Validation. Juan Ding: Formal analysis, Validation. Jie Sun: Formal analysis, Validation. Yang Liu: Formal analysis, Validation. Tingting Jiang: Investigation, Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We appreciate the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (ZR2020MC079, ZR2014BP004), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21505066), a project of Shandong Province Higher Educational Science and Technology Program, China (J18KA265).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2023.113207.
